judge_details.raw:
I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the constraints and quality criteria.

## Constraint Checklist

**Maximum 500 words:** All three appear within range. Proposal 1 is the longest due to its preamble and more verbose enforcement section.

**Exactly 4 sections:** All three have Scope, Permitted Uses, Prohibited Uses, Enforcement. ✓

**Numbered items (not prose paragraphs):** All three comply. ✓

**Every prohibition references motivating base fact:** All three include parenthetical citations. Proposal 2 and 3 are identical here and slightly stronger on Prohibited Use #3 (explicitly citing the copyright ownership finding, not just the license artifact incident).

**Enforceable without new tooling:** Proposal 1 **fails** here — Enforcement item 1 introduces a signed acknowledgment submission to HR, which is a new administrative mechanism not derivable from existing processes. Proposals 2 and 3 avoid this.

**No aspirational language:** All three comply. ✓

## Quality Analysis

**Proposal 1:** Has a freestanding preamble paragraph (violates "numbered items, not prose paragraphs" spirit and adds words). The HR acknowledgment process is a new mechanism. Scope item 2 asserts sales has no approved tools as a blanket rule, which is somewhat reasonable but less precise. Prohibited Use #3 covers only license artifacts, missing the copyright ownership risk flagged by counsel.

**Proposal 2:** Fixes the preamble and HR acknowledgment issues. Adds Prohibited Use #6 (Slack AI features) with an enforcement item, which is logically sound. Prohibited Use #3 is stronger, covering both license artifacts and copyright ownership. Permitted Use #3 handles non-engineering roles cleanly. Nearly identical to Proposal 3.

**Proposal 3:** Identical substantive content to Proposal 2 but includes a lengthy explanatory preamble explaining changes made — this meta-commentary is not part of the policy memo itself and doesn't affect the policy text. The actual policy document is identical to Proposal 2.

## Ranking

- **Proposal 2** and **Proposal 3** are substantively identical in policy text. Proposal 3's prefatory explanation is extraneous but doesn't degrade the policy itself.
- **Proposal 1** has a concrete constraint violation (new tooling/process in enforcement) and a weaker Prohibited Use #3.

RANKING: [2], [3], [1]

I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the constraints and quality criteria.

---

## Constraint Checklist

**Max 500 words:** All three appear within range. Proposal 1 is the longest due to its preamble and more verbose enforcement section.

**Exactly 4 sections (Scope, Permitted Uses, Prohibited Uses, Enforcement):** Proposal 1 has a freestanding preamble paragraph before the sections — this violates the "exactly 4 sections" constraint and introduces out-of-section prose. Proposals 2 and 3 comply cleanly.

**Numbered items (not prose paragraphs):** All three comply within sections. Proposal 1's preamble is a prose paragraph outside any section — a structural violation.

**Every prohibition must reference which base fact motivates it:** All three include parenthetical citations. Proposals 2 and 3 are stronger — they include the copyright ownership risk in Prohibition #3 (the outside counsel finding that AI-generated code may not be copyrightable), which Proposal 1 omits, leaving a gap against the base facts.

**Enforceable without new tooling:** Proposal 1 introduces an HR acknowledgment/sign-off process (Enforcement item 1) — this is a new administrative mechanism not derivable from existing access controls and review processes. This is a constraint violation. Proposals 2 and 3 rely solely on existing PR review, code review, and GitHub/Slack admin controls.

**No aspirational language:** All three comply.

---

## Specificity and Quality Analysis

**Proposal 1:**
- Structural violation: freestanding preamble paragraph
- Enforcement violation: introduces new HR acknowledgment process
- Incomplete prohibition #3: omits the copyright ownership/copyrightability finding from outside counsel
- Scope item 2 flatly states sales has "no approved tools" — this is accurate but handled more cleanly in P2/P3
- Prohibition #5 references "FedRAMP-scoped repositories" as if they're a pre-existing designated set, which isn't established in the base facts

**Proposal 2:**
- Clean four-section structure, no preamble
- No new tooling introduced
- Prohibition #3 correctly captures both the license artifact risk AND the copyright ownership risk
- Adds Prohibition #6 (Slack AI features) with enforcement item — this is well-grounded in base facts
- FedRAMP prohibition correctly triggers on CTO written designation rather than presupposed repository list
- Permitted Use #3 handles non-engineering roles accurately

**Proposal 3:**
- Substantively identical to Proposal 2 in the policy document itself
- Includes a lengthy preamble explaining changes — but this explanatory text is not part of the policy memo itself; it's editorial commentary
- The actual policy document is identical to Proposal 2 in all material respects
- The editorial section doesn't violate the memo constraints since it precedes the memo, but it adds no policy value

---

## Ranking

**Proposal 2** is the cleanest: no structural violations, no new tooling, complete fact citations, correct FedRAMP trigger, Slack prohibition included, no extraneous content.

**Proposal 3** produces an identical policy document to Proposal 2 but wraps it in unnecessary editorial commentary — marginally worse presentation, same policy quality.

**Proposal 1** has two clear constraint violations (preamble prose, new HR acknowledgment process) and an incomplete prohibition.

RANKING: [2], [3], [1]

I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the constraints and quality criteria.

## Constraint Checklist

**Max 500 words:** All three appear within range. Proposal 1 is the longest due to its preamble and more verbose enforcement section.

**Exactly 4 sections:** All three have Scope, Permitted Uses, Prohibited Uses, Enforcement. ✓ for all.

**Numbered items (not prose paragraphs):** All three use numbered items. ✓ for all. Proposal 3 has a prose preamble before the sections — wait, no, Proposal 3 removed the preamble. Proposal 1 retains a preamble paragraph. This is a constraint violation for Proposal 1.

**Every prohibition references motivating base fact:** All three include parenthetical citations. ✓ for all.

**Enforceable without new tooling:** Proposal 1's Enforcement item 1 (signed acknowledgment to HR, grace period) introduces a new administrative process not derivable from existing controls. This is a constraint violation. Proposals 2 and 3 avoid this.

**No aspirational language:** All three appear clean here. ✓

## Quality Analysis

**Proposal 1:** Has a freestanding preamble paragraph (not in a section, not numbered) — violates the numbered-items-not-prose constraint. Enforcement item 1 introduces a new HR acknowledgment mechanism — violates the no-new-tooling constraint. Scope item 2 asserts sales has no approved tools, which is an overreach. Missing Slack AI prohibition as a standalone item, making Enforcement item 7 weakly grounded.

**Proposal 2:** Cleaner structure. No preamble. Adds Slack AI as Prohibited Use #6, giving Enforcement item 6 proper grounding. Permitted Use #3 handles non-engineering roles without asserting facts beyond the base. Prohibited Use #3 covers both license artifacts AND copyright ownership gap (matching outside counsel's full finding). No new administrative mechanisms. Minor issue: Enforcement item 4 says "withholding access from any work the CTO has not designated" — slightly ambiguous but workable.

**Proposal 3:** Identical body to Proposal 2 but includes a lengthy prose preamble explaining editorial decisions. That preamble is not part of the policy memo itself — it's meta-commentary. The actual policy text is identical to Proposal 2. As a submitted memo, the meta-commentary is extraneous and unprofessional, but the policy content is the same quality.

## Ranking

- **Proposal 2** is the best clean policy memo: no constraint violations, proper structure, complete coverage, no extraneous content.
- **Proposal 3** has identical policy content to Proposal 2 but includes unprofessional meta-commentary that would not belong in a company memo.
- **Proposal 1** has two clear constraint violations (prose preamble outside sections, new HR acknowledgment mechanism) and a missing Slack prohibition.

RANKING: [2], [3], [1]
